title: Build your own web game — the Tower Duel notebook
meta.desc: An illustrated tutorial to understand how a video game that runs in a browser is built, using the game Tower Duel.
hero.title: …a real video game inside a browser
hero.lead: Follow the making of Tower Duel, a two-player cannon duel, step by step. With diagrams, real code, and little games you can play with to understand.
s1.lead: A web game is a game that runs in a browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari…) without installing anything. You click a link, and it plays!
s1.p1: For that to work, we put together three ingredients you may already know:
s1.li3: ⚙️ JavaScript — the brain: it's what makes things move.
s1.p2: Our game draws everything inside a special area called a canvas (a digital painter's “canvas”). We use a toolbox, Phaser, which handles the tricky parts (displaying, animating, listening to the keyboard) so we can focus on the game.
s1.fig.cap: From code to the screen: JavaScript draws the game inside the canvas, and you play.
s1.callout: Remember. The browser already knows how to display pages. A web game is “just” a very lively page where JavaScript redraws the image dozens of times per second.
s2.p1: Two towers, one on each side of a landscape. Each player sets the angle and the power of their cannon, taking the wind into account. The twist: both players aim at the same time, then both shots fire together. The first to hit the opposite tower wins the round!
s2.p2: We built the game in 4 steps (we call them “batches”). Each chapter tells one step, with its own biome mood:
s2.c1.title: Make a cannon move
s2.c1.desc: The game loop, the path of a cannonball, and how we “wrap up” the game with Docker.
s2.c2.title: Make it look good
s2.c2.desc: Scenery (biomes), particles, depth (parallax) and sound effects made by code.
s2.c3.title: Play across several screens
s2.c3.desc: The TV displays, the phones control. A server referees the match in real time.
s2.c4.title: Destroy the landscape
s2.c4.desc: Like in Worms: every shot digs a crater. We'll see the magic trick to “punch a hole” in an image.
s3.p1: Every chapter comes in three depths. Read the one that fits — or climb from one to the next as your curiosity grows. (The level switcher sits at the top of each chapter, so you can jump anytime.)
s3.l1.title: Start here
s3.l1.desc: The friendly tour, with diagrams and little games to play with. No code background needed.
s3.l2.title: The architecture
s3.l2.desc: How the project is actually structured: the game loop, scenes, the simulation/rendering split, the protocol.
s3.l3.title: In depth
s3.l3.desc: The engine internals: numerical integration, determinism, netcode, procedural synthesis and fast maths.
s4.p1: You'll come across little coloured boxes. Here's what they mean:
s4.callout.tip: Remember — the important idea to keep in mind.
s4.callout.try: Try it! — a little game right below, to play with using your mouse or finger.
s4.callout.nerd: For the curious — a sharper detail, if you want to dig deeper (you can skip it!).
s4.p2: And blocks of real game code, like this one:
s4.code.c1: // once both players have confirmed, we fire!
s5.p1: Extras to go further: deploy the game yourself, a detour through the maths, and the behind-the-scenes of the starting brief.
s5.li1.label: Deployment
s5.li1.desc: — build the Docker image and host the game online.
s5.li2.label: Quick maths
s5.li2.desc: — the square root, Quake III's 0x5f3759df code, and Pythagoras.
s5.li3.label: The starting brief
s5.li3.desc: — the spec that started it all, annotated and critiqued.
footer.suffix: made for curious minds of all ages 🛠️
